data.description:

Approach 1: Himax Technologies Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes projected future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, aiming to estimate what the entire stream of cash is worth right now.

For Himax Technologies, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $122.29 million. Simply Wall St includes an explicit forecast for 2025 free cash flow of $40.7 million and then extends the picture using ten year projections that range around $119 million to $144 million a year through 2035, with each of those future amounts discounted back to today.

Bringing all those discounted cash flows together gives an estimated intrinsic value of US$6.56 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about US$11.64, the DCF output points to the stock trading around 77.3% above this estimate, which indicates a relatively rich valuation under this model.

Result: OVERVALUED

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to link what you pay today to the earnings the business is already generating. It helps you see how many years of current earnings the market is effectively pricing into each share.

A higher or lower P/E often reflects what investors expect for future growth and how much risk they are willing to accept. Faster growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher “normal” P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually leads to a lower one.

Himax Technologies currently trades on a P/E of 46.21x. This sits just under the Semiconductor industry average of about 48.04x and below the peer group average of 71.95x. data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Havas (ENXTAM:HAVAS) After Recent Share Price Swings?
data.description:
• Wondering if Havas at €16.20 is priced for opportunity or already reflecting the story? This article walks you through what the numbers suggest about value.
  • The stock has seen mixed returns lately, with a 0.9% decline over the last 7 days, an 8.6% gain over 30 days, and an 18.8% return over the past year, while year to date it is down 5.4%.
  • Recent coverage of Havas has focused on its position within the media sector and how investors are reassessing the stock after these price moves. This has sparked fresh interest from readers who want to understand whether the current share price still lines up with the underlying fundamentals.

Havas delivered 18.8% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Media industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Havas Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting them back to today so they are in the same euros as the current share price.

For Havas, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about €327.6 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections used in the model include free cash flow of €291.97 million in 2026 and €319 million in 2030, all in €. After 2030, Simply Wall St extends the series using modest growth estimates to complete a ten year cash flow path.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back, the model suggests an intrinsic value of about €72.47 per share. Compared with a current share price of €16.20, that implies the stock trades at a 77.6% discount to this DCF estimate, which indicates a wide potential valuation gap.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Havas Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Havas, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings that each share represents, which is why many investors lean on it as a primary yardstick.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends heavily on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually go with a lower one.

Havas currently trades on a P/E of 8.39x. That is below both the Media industry average of 16.73x and the peer group average of 21.11x. Simply Wall St’s proprietary Fair Ratio for Havas is 10.47x. This Fair Ratio is designed to reflect the P/E that might be reasonable given the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, risk factors, industry and market cap, rather than relying solely on broad industry or peer comparisons.

Because the Fair Ratio builds these company specific factors into a single yardstick, it can be a more tailored reference point than raw peer or sector averages. Comparing 8.39x to the Fair Ratio of 10.47x suggests Havas trades below this tailored benchmark.

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of how much cash a company could generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to today, aiming to express all those future dollars as a single present value per share.

data.title: Volkswagen’s China EV Pivot Tests Export Strategy And Xpeng Partnership
data.description:
• Volkswagen is increasing exports of China made electric vehicles to emerging markets as part of a broader reset of its China plans.
  • The company is expanding its collaboration with Chinese EV maker Xpeng, including joint development work and technology sharing.
  • Volkswagen has outlined plans to introduce 50 plug in hybrid and electric models in China, signaling a larger focus on locally adapted products.

For investors watching XTRA:VOW3, this China push comes as the share price sits at about €89.98 and longer term returns have been mixed. The stock is down 15.5% year to date and 38.3% over five years, while the one year return is positive at 3.8%. Those figures frame how much is riding on Volkswagen’s ability to reposition in its largest automotive market.

The expanded export push and the deeper tie up with Xpeng show Volkswagen repositioning its China operations around electric and plug in hybrid models that can also serve other regions. For readers, the key questions will be how efficiently the company can scale these partnerships and products, and whether the China based EV strategy can translate into more stable performance over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Volkswagen by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Volkswagen.

📰 Beyond the headline: 4 risks and 4 things going right for Volkswagen that every investor should see.

For Volkswagen, using China as both a product development hub and an export base is a clear attempt to reshape how the group competes with local EV leaders like BYD and Nio, while also defending share against Tesla and other global brands. Exporting China made EVs to emerging markets can help absorb excess capacity in a softer Chinese market and spread fixed costs over more units, which matters for a group still working through high investment needs and mixed long term returns. The deeper collaboration with Xpeng also signals that Volkswagen is leaning on local software and EV platforms rather than relying only on in house solutions. This may shorten product cycles and keep features closer to local preferences. The trade off is higher execution risk, as more joint ventures, more brands and more regional product lines can add complexity in an organisation that analysts already see as complicated and capital intensive.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Volkswagen Narrative

  • The China export push and Xpeng partnership align with the narrative that Volkswagen is using local production, partnerships and cost optimisation to reduce tariff and geopolitical risk while supporting margin recovery.
  • At the same time, heavier reliance on Chinese partners and EV capacity feeds into existing concerns about pressure from battery electric vehicle competition in China and Europe, which can weigh on mix and profitability.
  • The use of China made EVs for emerging markets and the Xpeng tie up adds another layer to the group’s joint venture and software strategy, which may not be fully captured in high level views that focus mainly on tariffs and U.S. partnerships.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Volkswagen to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 4 key risks, including concerns that Volkswagen’s debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, which could be more challenging if China focused EV investments take longer to pay off.
  • ⚠️ Heightened EV competition in China and softer demand raise the risk that exporting surplus production simply shifts pressure to other markets rather than easing margin strain.
  • 🎁 Volkswagen screens as trading well below some fair value estimates and is viewed as good value compared with peers. Efficient use of lower cost China production could support that value case if executed carefully.
  • 🎁 The partnership model, including Xpeng and other technology collaborations, aligns with efforts to improve product quality and software offerings without bearing all development costs alone.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on how quickly China developed EVs reach meaningful volumes outside China and whether pricing holds up in those emerging markets. Watch for more detail from management on profitability of China based exports versus European plants, and how Xpeng related models are received by consumers at events such as major auto shows in Beijing. It is also worth tracking whether analysts adjust revenue or margin expectations as the 50 model China plan progresses, especially given the existing mix of risks and rewards already identified for Volkswagen.

Approach 1: HP Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back into today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what all the future cash HP might generate is worth right now.

HP’s latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $2.97b. Analysts have provided forecasts out to 2030, with Simply Wall St extending those into a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model using cash flow projections through 2035. For example, projected Free Cash Flow in 2030 is $3.08b, and the model discounts each year’s figure back to today using a required rate of return.

When all those discounted cash flows are added together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $42.80 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $21.09, the DCF output implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 50.7%. This suggests HP may be trading at a substantial discount to this cash flow based estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: HP Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like HP, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market is baking in for future growth and how much risk investors feel they are taking on.

Stronger growth and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tend to point to a lower “normal” or “fair” P/E range. That is why it helps to check HP’s current P/E against a few benchmarks rather than looking at the number in isolation.

HP currently trades on a P/E of 7.69x. The broader Tech industry average P/E is 23.70x, and the peer group average is 47.58x, so HP’s earnings multiple sits well below both of these reference points. Simply Wall St also calculates a “Fair Ratio” of 20.25x, which is the P/E that might be expected given HP’s earnings growth profile, margins, industry, market cap and risk factors.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple industry or peer comparison because it adjusts for company specific characteristics rather than assuming all tech names deserve the same multiple. Comparing the Fair Ratio of 20.25x with the actual P/E of 7.69x suggests the shares trade below this earnings based reference point.
